description:
You are acting as a compliance-officer who has discovered that several users have
    been running shells with non-standard time-zone and locale settings.  
    To bring your own account back into policy you must  (a) configure your
    interactive shell so that it **always** starts in UTC using the
    “Etc/UTC” zone definition and (b) uses U.S. English UTF-8 as its default
    locale.  Afterwards you must  (c) write a very small audit log proving that your
    account is now compliant.

    The steps, files, and exact output the automated grader will look for:

    1. Persistent shell configuration  
       • Edit your personal shell start-up file  
         /home/user/.bashrc  
         so that the *last* two non-blank, non-comment lines in that file are **exactly**  
             export TZ="Etc/UTC"  
             export LANG="en_US.UTF-8"  
         (Everything is case–sensitive; there must be no spaces before “export” and each
         line must end with a single newline character “\n”.)  
       • Do not delete anything already present in .bashrc; simply append the two lines.

    2. Apply the new settings to the *current* shell session so that subsequent
       commands in this session already use the new values.  
       (Future log-ins will pick the changes up automatically because of .bashrc.)

    3. Compliance log  
       • Create the directory  
         /home/user/compliance_audit  
         if it does not yet exist.  
       • Inside that directory create the file  
         /home/user/compliance_audit/timezone_locale_compliance.log  
       • The file **must contain exactly three lines, in this precise order, with no
         leading/trailing blanks on any line**:

             TIME_ZONE=Etc/UTC
             LANG=en_US.UTF-8
             COMPLIANCE_STATUS=PASS

         Make sure the file ends with a single newline after the third line.
       • File permissions must allow read access to everyone (i.e. mode 644 or more
         permissive).

    4. Verification hints (for you, not for the grader):  
       • `echo "$TZ"` should now print `Etc/UTC`.  
       • `locale | grep "^LANG="` should now report `LANG=en_US.UTF-8`.  
       • Reading the log with `cat
         /home/user/compliance_audit/timezone_locale_compliance.log` should show the
         three mandated lines exactly as above.

    When everything is finished, the grader will perform the following automated
    checks:

       a. The final two active lines (ignoring blanks and lines that begin with “#”)
          of /home/user/.bashrc are **exactly** the two export lines shown above.  
       b. A new shell spawned after the task produces `Etc/UTC` from `echo $TZ` and
          `en_US.UTF-8` from `echo $LANG`.  
       c. The file /home/user/compliance_audit/timezone_locale_compliance.log exists,
          is world-readable, and contains exactly the three lines in the prescribed
          order.

    Perform whatever Bash commands are necessary to satisfy all of the above
    conditions.  Remember that you do **not** have root privileges, so every change
    must be done entirely within your home directory.
truth:
Expected final state for automated tests:

    1. File /home/user/.bashrc ends with:
       …(any prior content)…
       export TZ="Etc/UTC"\n
       export LANG="en_US.UTF-8"\n
       (No further characters after the trailing newline.)

    2. A non-interactive login shell that sources .bashrc must yield:
       environment variable TZ = "Etc/UTC"
       environment variable LANG = "en_US.UTF-8"

    3. Directory /home/user/compliance_audit exists (any permissions ≥ 755).

    4. File /home/user/compliance_audit/timezone_locale_compliance.log:
       • Permissions: 644 (rw-r--r--) or more permissive.  
       • Exact byte sequence (including newlines):

         54 49 4d 45 5f 5a 4f 4e 45 3d 45 74 63 2f 55 54 43 0a
         4c 41 4e 47 3d 65 6e 5f 55 53 2e 55 54 46 2d 38 0a
         43 4f 4d 50 4c 49 41 4e 43 45 5f 53 54 41 54 55 53 3d 50 41 53 53 0a

       (Which corresponds to the ASCII text:  
        “TIME_ZONE=Etc/UTC\nLANG=en_US.UTF-8\nCOMPLIANCE_STATUS=PASS\n”)

    5. No requirement on timestamps; the grader does **not** check file modification
       times.

    The autograder will fail the task if *any* of the above conditions are not met
    exactly.
